blocks.text: ELSEVIER
Introducing the reduced aspect graph
Aldo Laurentini
Dipartimento di Automatica e Informatica, Politecnico di Torino, Corso Duca degli Abruzzi 24, 10125 Torino, Italy
Received 27 September 1993
Abstract
Aspect graphs (AGs) have been proposed as viewer centered representations of 3D objects, suitable for recognizing objects
from single or multiple views. Many algorithms for computing the AGs of various categories of objects have been presented;
however, very few AG-based recognition applications have been developed. One reason for the limited practical use of AGs is
the impressive growth of the information stored as the complexity of the object increases. This may imply comparing the views
of the unknown object with a very high number of stored views, and therefore a time-consuming recognition process. The pur-
pose of this paper is to introduce a new kind of aspect graph representation, the reduced aspect graph (RAG). RAGs are suitable
for topological object recognition from single or multiple views, and require less information to be stored. Therefore, the match-
ing process is shortened.
Keywords: Line drawings; Aspect graphs; Topological matching; Reduced aspect graph
1. Introduction
When computer vision is aimed at recognizing
known objects in a 3D environment, a popular ap-
proach consists in storing a number of possible ap-
pearances of the objects. This should allow recogni-
tion of an object by matching one or more views of
the object to the stored views.
A notable development along this line is the aspect
graph (AG), proposed first by Koenderink and van
Doorn (1979). The Ag concerns views consisting of
line drawings generated by projecting onto the image
plane the edges of polyhedral objects, or the creases
(surface normal discontinuities) or limbs (depth
discontinuities) of general objects. Two ideas are at
the basis of the the AG concept. The first idea is clus-
tering the infinite possible views into a set of repre-
sentative views, the aspects, according to their topo-
logical equivalence. The second idea is the topological
adjacency of the aspects. The AG is a graph structure
where topologically distinct views, i.e. aspects, are
stored at the vertices, and the arcs connecting topo-
logically adjacent aspects represent topological
changes, or visual events, or transitions.
According to recent research, human perception
may involve an AG-like internal representation of
objects. References to neurophysiological work on this
subject, as well as other topics concerning AGs, can
be found in a recent paper by Seibert and Waxman
Each aspect of an object corresponds to a charac-
teristic zone of the viewing space. The graph structure
of the AG is the dual graph of the partition into char-
acteristic zones of the viewing space. If parallel as-
pects, obtained from parallel projections of the ob-
ject, are considered, the viewing space consists of ideal
points. In this case, a useful representation of the
viewing space is the Gaussian sphere. Algorithms for
computing the partition of the viewing space under
parallel or perspective projection have been given for
polygons, convex and general polyhedra, solids of
revolution and other curved objects. For further de-
tails on this matter, the interested reader is referred
to the above cited paper by Seibert and Waxman
(1992), the survey paper by Bowyer and Dyer (1990)
and the work by Gigus et al. (1991).
Although the bibliography on AGs today includes
dozens of papers, relatively little work has been re-
ported on AG-based recognition algorithms. In par-
ticular, very little work fully exploits the information
stored, i.e. aspects and transitions. This information
suggests a multiple views recognition approach, where
we match a sequence of aspects and transitions of the
unknown object to the stored information.
An important drawback of the AG approach is the
number of aspects and transitions that must be stored,
even for relatively simple objects. Let us consider for
instance polyhedral convex objects with \(n\) faces. If the
planes supporting the faces are in general position
(i.e., the intersection of each triplet of planes is a
point, and all these points are different), the number
of perspective aspects is
and the number of transitions is
where
\(K(n, d)=\sum_{i=0}^{d}\binom{n}{i}\)
is the number of zones in which a hyperspace with \(d\)
dimensions is divided by \(n\) hyperplanes, for \(n>d\) (see
(Gruenbaum, 1967, p. 393)). The numbers of as-
pects and transitions of the parallel AG are
\(2+n(n+1)\) and \(2 n(n+1)\), respectively. For in-
stance, a simple convex polyhedron with ten faces has
a perspective AG with 175 aspects and 450 transi-
tions. If the faces are not in general position, the above
formulae supply upper bounds. Formulae giving the
number of aspects of the case of pairs of parallel faces
are reported by Laurentini (1990). The numbers of
aspects can sharply increase with \(n\) for concave poly-
hedra. Worst case polyhedral scenes, such that nodes
and arcs of the parallel and perspective AG are \(\mathrm{O}\left(n^{6}\right)\)
and \(\mathrm{O}\left(n^{9}\right)\) respectively, have been presented by
Plantinga and Dyer (1990). This could imply com-
paring the views of an unknown object with a very
high number of stored aspects.
The purpose of this paper is to introduce a new kind
of aspect graph representation which is convenient
for topological recognition from multiple images and
requires a reduced number of aspects and transi-
tions, therefore shortening the matching process.
2. Recognition through topological matching of
multiple views
The aspect graph idea is essentially topological. Al-
though some researchers also store metrical infor-
mation at the nodes of the aspect graph, for instance
Chakravarty and Freeman (1982), in many papers
an aspect is topologically defined as the graph struc-
ture of a representative line drawing. Referring to po-
lyhedra, the arcs of this graph structure are labeled
with the names of the edges of the polyhedron (Gigus
et al., 1991), or marks indicating convex, concave or
occluding edges (Gigus and Malik, 1990). Labeling
the arcs of the graph structure of a line drawing with
the names of the corresponding edges of the polyhed-
ron is an implicit way for specifying also the labels of
the arcs of the AG, i.e. the visual events.
Recognition through a topological match of un-
known object images and stored aspects appears to
be a natural use of AGs. Should a topological match
be insufficient for full identification, it could be fol-
lowed by the analysis of some other feature of the un-
known object.
However, it is easy to see that in many cases the
information stored in an AG is redundant for this
purpose. For instance, let us consider the topological
recognition of a simple polyhedron, a tetrahedron,
using its AG. The AG of a tetrahedron is shown in
Fig. 1. In this case, the parallel and perspective AGs
are coincident, and there are 14 aspects. Since we deal
with a convex polyhedron, we can specify the aspects
as the sets of visible faces. If we consider, from a top-
ological point of view, a line drawing extracted from
an image of the tetrahedon, it is clear that we are not
able to tell one face from the other (for a regular tet-
rahedron this happens even if we use metrical infor-
mation). Let us suppose that the current view of the
Fig. 1. A tetrahedron and its AG. The nodes of the AG have been labeled with the indices of the visible faces.
tetrahedron to be recognized supplies a line drawing
like that shown in Fig. 2(a), and let us compare its
topological structure with the aspects of the AG. We
find four aspects which match this line drawing, that
is, the four aspects containing three faces. Even if we
use a sequence of aspects and transitions extracted
from multiple images, this ambiguity cannot be re-
solved. By suitably displacing the viewpoint with re-
spect to the object, we can obtain a transition toward
a line drawing like that in Fig. 2(b). From this, dis-
placing the viewpoint again, we can obtain a transi-
tion either toward a line drawing like (c) or again
like (a), and so on. It is easy to see that any such
sequence obtained from the object to be recognized
matches several sequences of aspects and transitions
of the AG, starting from any of the four aspects con-
taining three faces. The example shows that the top-
ological information stored in the AG of the tetrahe-
dron is redundant: it can be reduced to the following:
(i) there are three topologically distinct aspects,
corresponding to the line drawings in Fig. 2(a), (b)
and (c);
Fig. 2. The three line drawings of a tetrahedron which are topo-
logically different.
(ii) transitions are possible between the pairs of
aspects \([(\mathrm{a})(\mathrm{b})]\) and \([(\mathrm{b})(\mathrm{c})]\).
3. The reduced aspect graph
The example discussed in the previous section sug-
gests the idea of reduced aspect graph (RAG) of a
polyhedron. The RAG can be obtained as follows
from the full AG. Consider any pair of nodes in AG
and merge them together if the aspects they are la-
beled with are topologically equivalent. The arcs in-
cident at the two original nodes become arcs incident
at the unique resulting node. If two arcs joining the
same nodes are obtained, delete one of them. Each
aspect of the RAG represents a category of topologi-
cally equivalent aspects of AG. The RAG nodes must
be labeled with a topological description of the aspect
without reference to the edges or faces of the origi-
nating polyhedron. A description of aspect suitable
for topological matching can be given in terms of ad-
jacent polygons (Laurentini, 1993).
It is clear that the reduced aspect graph has the fol-
lowing properties:
(a) for each aspect \(A\) in \(A G\) there is one aspect \(R A\)
topologically equivalent in RAG;
(b) for each transition connecting two aspects \(A^{\prime}\) and
\(A^{\prime \prime}\) in \(A G\), there is in RAG a transition connecting the
aspects of the topologically equivalent pair \(R A^{\prime}\) and
\(R A^{\prime \prime}\).
This guarantees that any sequence of aspects and
transitions which can be found in the full AG of a po-
lyhedron can be found also in its RAG. Thus, if we
Fig. 3. A pentahedron and its AG . The nodes of the Ag are labeled with the indices of the visible faces.
Fig. 4. The reduced aspect graph of the pentahedron. Each node
has been labeled with a representative line drawing of the cate-
gory of topologically equivalent line drawings which form the
aspect.
Fig. 5. The reduced aspect graph of a cube.
want to topologically recognize a polyhedron by ex-
tracting a sequence of aspects and transitions from
multiple images, we can use the RAG instead of the
complete AG.
Let us now determine the RAG for some simple
polyhedra. Consider first a pentahedron. If the planes
supporting the faces are in general position, from for-
mulae (1) and (2) of Section 2 we obtain that the
perspective AG has 25 nodes and 50 edges. The com-
plete AG is shown in Fig. 3.
From this AG, by merging together the topologi-
cally equivalent aspects, we obtain the RAG shown
in Fig. 4. In this figure, the aspects relative to each
node have been specified by one representative line
drawing. The RAG contains eight aspects and nine
transitions, a substantial reduction with respect to the
complete AG.
An even larger reduction can be obtained for non-
general polyhedra. Let us consider a cube. Its per-
spective AG (which is coincident with the parallel
AG) contains 26 aspects, as can be obtained from the
formulae giving the number of aspects for the case of
polyhedra with pairs of parallel faces (Laurentini,
1990). An image of the complete AG of a cube can
be found in the survey paper by Besl and Jain (1985)
on three-dimensional object recognition. It is easy to
see that the RAG of the cube contains only three as-
pects and two transitions, as shown in Fig. 5.
4. The discriminating power of AG and RAG
We have seen that any sequence of aspects and
transitions which can be found in the regular AG of
a polyhedron can be also found in its RAG. Should
we conclude that all the topological information of
an AG is resumed by the corresponding RAG? Ac-
tually, this is not the case, as we will show with the
following example.
Let us consider the full AG of the esahedron \(\boldsymbol{P}\) of
Fig. 6(a).
Let \(p_{i}\) be the plane supporting the face \(f_{i}\). The inter-
section of \(\boldsymbol{P}\) and plane \(p\), normal to the back face \(f_{4}\)
and containing the intersection \(L\) of \(p_{1}\) and \(p_{3}\) is shown
in Fig. 6(b). In this figure, the section of the un-
bounded characteristic zone \(c z\) is also shown. From
the viewpoints belonging to \(c z\) we can observe the as-
Fig. 6. An esatedron \(\boldsymbol{P}\) (a), and its section (b) made with the plane \(p\) normal to the back face \(f_{4}\) and passing through line \(L\), the intersec-
tion of the planes supporting the side faces \(f_{1}\) and \(f_{3}\). In (c) the section of the esahedron \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\), obtained from \(\boldsymbol{P}\) by displacing plane \(p_{6}\)
supporting face \(f_{6}\), is shown.
Fig. 7. The RAG of both \(\boldsymbol{P}\) and \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\).
pect with five faces \(\left\{f_{1}, f_{2}, f_{3}, f_{4}, f_{6}\right\} . c z\) is bounded by
the three planes \(p_{1}, p_{3}\), and \(p_{4}\). Since crossing one of
these planes makes the corresponding face invisible,
this aspect is adjacent to three aspects with four faces:
Consider now polyhedron \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\), obtained from \(\boldsymbol{P}\) by
displacing plane \(p_{6}\). The intersection of \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\) and \(p\) is
shown in Fig. 6(c). In this case, the zone \(c z^{*}\), relative
to the aspect with five faces \(\left\{f_{1}, f_{2}, f_{3}, f_{4}, f_{6}\right\}\), is bounded
by the four planes \(p_{1}, p_{3}, p_{4}\) and \(p_{6}\). Therefore it is
adjacent to four aspects with four faces: \(\left\{f_{2}, f_{3}, f_{4}, f_{6}\right\}\),
\(\boldsymbol{P}\) and \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\) have only one aspect with five faces, adja-
cent to three aspects in one case and to four aspects
in the other, their AGs are different.
Let us derive the RAGs of both \(\boldsymbol{P}\) and \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\). It is easy
to see that they are equal. In both cases, the aspects
adjacent to the aspect with five faces are topologi-
cally equivalent, and thus are reduced to one aspect
in the RAG (see Fig. 7).
In conclusion, we have shown that two polyhedra
having different AGs could have the same RAG, and
therefore the AG could have a greater capability than
the RAG for discriminating objects topologically.
However, it should be observed that this capability
cannot be exploited under our recognition paradigm,
consisting of : (i) extracting a sequence of aspects and
transitions from an image of the object in relative
motion with respect to the viewpoint; (ii) topologi-
cally matching this sequence to the stored informa-
tion. In the example presented, for telling \(\boldsymbol{P}\) from \(\boldsymbol{P}^{\prime}\),
we should be able to explore the full AG of the un-
known object. In particular, we should be able to find
how many topologically equivalent aspects are adja-
cent to the aspect with five faces. For performing such
tasks the viewpoint should be driven by some active
algorithm based on metric properties of the object, in
contrast with the simple topological approach
assumed.
5. Discussion
We have introduced a new kind of aspect graph
representation, the reduced aspect graph, which is
suitable for the topological identification of polyhe-
dral objects from multiple views. The main feature
of the RAG is that any sequence of aspects and tran-
sitions which can be found in the full AG of a poly-
hedron can be also found in its RAG.
Although the examples presented in this paper re-
fer to convex polyhedra, the idea of reduced aspect
graph can be extended to general polyhedra or even
to objects with curved surfaces. The rationale of the
RAG idea is that it requires storing a reduced num-
ber of views with respect to the AG, and therefore the
identification of an object can be performed with a
reduced number of comparisons. A substantial re-
duction of the information stored has been verified
for some simple polyhedra.
The RAG idea raises a number of problems. First
observe that the algorithm described in Section 3 for
computing the RAG requires \(\mathrm{O}\left(m^{2}\right)\) comparisons for
an AG with \(m\) aspects. This means \(\mathrm{O}\left(n^{18}\right)\) compari-
sons for the perpective RAG of a non-convex poly-
hedron. Thus it should be only considered an ideal
procedure, and in practice more efficient algorithms
are needed. A possible approach consists of modify-
ing an algorithm for computing the full AG, like the
algorithm of Gigus et al. (1991) for computing the
parallel AG of general polyhedra. Each new aspect
generated by this algorithm should be compared with
the previously generated aspects; many comparisons
could be pruned using some suitable data
organization.
For a better understanding of the advantages pro-
vided by the RAG it would be important to derive
the average and worst-case complexities of the re-
duced aspect graph. These problems, though re-
stricted to convex polyhdra, do not appear to have
trivial solutions.
References
